narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels personal and unfair, and I'm truly sorry it's landed this way. Our prediction model looked at your full story and estimated a 40.9% chance you'd struggle to pass the bar exam down the line, which tipped the scales against admission right now. The biggest piece of your picture was your Year 3 decile of 2, placing you in the lower ranks among peers at that stage, and that stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward potential challenges ahead. Right behind it was your LSAT score of 32, which the model saw as another key hurdle compared to what typically succeeds in powering through to bar passage. Your Year 1 decile of 2 echoed that early pattern, reinforcing a sense that academic momentum might be tough to build. Even with your upper-middle family income, undergraduate GPA of 2.9, and being a full-time white male student aiming for law, these factors all nudged the outlook toward higher risk in the model's eyes, each layering on to paint a tougher road. It's not that any one thing defines you—it's how they combined to suggest a steeper climb than we can confidently back at this point. You've got target and drive written all over your profile, and that full-time commitment shows real dedication. This isn't a forever no; it's a now pause, giving you space to strengthen those early signals—like boosting test prep or grades—that the model weighs so heavily. Imagine retelling your story in a year with a higher decile or LSAT, and doors could swing wide open. Hang in there; your path to law isn't closed, just asking for a sharper edge first. We're rooting for your comeback.
